title: Penn State wrestling's Masanosuke Ono to wrestle at RAF12
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Masanosuke Ono will wrestle for the first time since joining Penn State last year.

Real American Freestyle announced Friday that Ono will face Ohio State's Ben Davino at RAF12, continuing the Ohio State-Penn State rivalry.

He'll join former Penn State star and current UFC middleweight Bo Nickal on the card in Cleveland. Nickal is set to face Max McEnelly in a light heavyweight bout.

Ono joined Penn State already holding an impressive international competition status. The Matsue City, Japan, native earned gold at both the 2024 Senior World Championships and U20 World Championships at 61 kilograms.

Prior to the start of last season, Cael Sanderson said Ono had one year of eligibility for the Nittany Lions. With the 133-pounder not wrestling last year, his matchup against Davino could be the start to eventually seeing him in a Penn State singlet for the 2026-27 dual season.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: 2 former Penn State men's basketball players make NBA Summer League rosters
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Two former Nittany Lions will be featured in the NBA Summer League this month.

Wing Seth Lundy made the summer league roster for the Toronto Raptors, and guard Ace Baldwin Jr. is a part of the Orlando Magic's summer league team.

Lundy wasn't on any NBA or G League roster this past season, and he was most recently waived from a two-way contract with the Los Angeles Clippers in July 2025. He spent time with the Atlanta Hawks and the Clippers during the 2024-25 regular season but didn't appear in any game due to injury.

Lundy was selected in the second round of the 2023 NBA draft by the Hawks, who he played in nine games with during 2023-24. He finished his four-year Penn State career in 2023 after helping the team make a run to the second round of the NCAA Tournament.

The Raptors' summer league schedule features games against the Boston Celtics on July 10, the Houston Rockets on July 11, the Indiana Pacers on July 13 and the Miami Heat on July 16 plus a to be determined fifth game at a later date.

Baldwin is coming off his first season as a professional player, appearing in nine games with the Magic's G League affiliate, the Osceola Magic. He was the No. 3 overall pick in the 2025 G League draft to the Capital City Go-Go, but his draft rights were dealt to the Magic.

Baldwin participated in last year's NBA Summer League as well, playing in two games with the Celtics. He was a two-time Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year during his Penn State career from 2023-25.

During the summer league, the Magic will face the Charlotte Hornets on July 9, the Miami Heat on July 11, the Portland Trail Blazers on July 12 and the Philadelphia 76ers on July 15.

title: How the FBS Oversight Committee’s proposed changes for college football would affect Penn State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s been a busy week for Penn State — making the switch from Nike to Adidas, hosting Lift for Life on Wednesday and continuing to move through summer workouts.

But it was also busy for the FBS Oversight Committee, who made several new proposals to impact future FBS seasons.

The biggest of the proposed changes is the calendar for the offseason, as the monetizing college football landscape tries to become more similar to professional sports. However, it also added additional proposals into recruiting and instant replay review.

The proposals were introduced last week and will either be denied or approved in a final committee vote in August. If they pass, they will take effect in 2027.

Here’s a look into each of the rules and their impact on both Penn State and the college football world entirely.

Transfer portal window

This was the first offseason with just one transfer portal window after the NCAA eliminated the spring window, but with this proposal, even more changes to one of the most important parts of the offseason would occur.

The new schedule ultimately modifies each period of the offseason, including the transfer portal window, the spring practice period and preseason training.

Under the proposed legislation, the transfer portal period would move from 15 days to 10 days. The window will open the next business day after Jan. 1, which is Jan. 4 in 2027.

Despite the shortened window, exceptions would still be made for teams who are still competing during or after the period. These players would have five days after the end of their season to enter, and players on teams with coaching changes would get 15 days to enter.

Over the last few years, Penn State had several key players depart via the transfer portal. In 2024, backup quarterback Beau Pribula entered the portal before Penn State’s College Football Playoffs semifinal run, and wide receiver Harrison Wallace III entered a month later.

If the new rules were in place, none of those transfers would’ve happened during the official window.

New spring schedule

With the evolution of NIL pushing college football draws even closer to professional sports, the offseason schedule is expected to take on a similar format.

Teams currently conduct 15 spring practices, culminating in a spring game for many teams, including Penn State.

Instead, there would be two periods of spring practice for a total of 21 on-field practices. The two periods have to be completed within seven weeks and neither window can be over five weeks long.

In addition to the mandatory practice window, teams would designate nine weeks for voluntary practice.

Between the seven weeks of two practice periods and additional nine weeks of voluntary practice, Penn State and other teams will have the opportunity to space out its practice while still utilizing the offseason.

This year, the additional periods would’ve given the Nittany Lions more practice with its large transfer class and spaced out the practices for injury prevention and recovery. While Tony Rojas, Rocco Becht, Brett Eskildsen and others were rehabbing during a majority of the spring, a second practice window could’ve given them an opportunity to practice later on.

Preseason changes

As a result of an increased spring practice schedule, the preseason is expected to be reduced.

Under the proposed guidelines, the preseason would feature 21 practices during 27 days. This is four days less practice than there is now in four less days.

Since teams still have more practices under the new proposals than previously, this further spaces out the offseason and preseason practices. In turn, teams would have more rest immediately before the season.

Other updates

While no official proposal was made, there were discussions on standardizing off-campus recruiting, providing a yearly allotment of days in which the coaching staff could go off-campus to recruit. It would permit up to 16 staff members and the head coach to participate in off-campus recruiting.

The FBS committee is expected to introduce the proposal during its July or August meeting, which would then go into effect in Jan. 2027 if passed. The only change that would take place for the 2026 season is an experimental rule where instant replay would be made available in the coach’s booth in real time.

Each conference will have the opportunity to implement it during the entirety of the conference season, while usage during nonconference games will be decided by the visiting team.

The experimental rule will play a big part in a coach’s decision to challenge a play, as coaches will now have the same view in the booth as a referee before issuing the challenge.

title: ‘It’s going to be a magical evening’ | Corner Room celebrates 100-year anniversary
author: Justin Elliott | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Corner Room restaurant will be celebrating its 100-year anniversary as a State College staple on Friday with “Corner Fest,” featuring a number of events in collaboration with other Hotel State College businesses and borough organizations.

The restaurant was founded in 1855 as Jack’s Road House, when Penn State was still the Farmers’ High School of Pennsylvania, and renamed in 1926. The Corner Room has been under new ownership since 2019, when Pat Croce & Company bought the parent company, Hotel State College & Co.

Stefania Albegiani, the lead server, has been with the restaurant for nearly a decade.

She worked for her dad growing up before opening up a pizzeria with her sister in the early 2000s. The two went their separate ways years ago, and Albegiani, who described herself as always having been a “restaurant girl,” began working at The Corner Room.

“Heartbroken, I found my way to my forever place. That was my blessing in disguise,” Albegiani said.

Albegiani witnessed the restaurant’s makeover under Pat Croce & Company during COVID. She looks through old photos of the restaurant “constantly,” and tells her coworkers about how much it has changed.

She said the improvements they made to the building set it up for many years of success, and the new leadership has been the best thing for The Corner Room.

While working at the restaurant, Albegiani said she has gotten both “old time” guests who come in to reminisce and newcomers passing through.

“I love to hear their stories. Some got engaged in our booths, while some grew up in the area and have been coming in since (they were) young children,” she said.

She has served Ty Burrell and JJ Abrams, both Penn State alums, as well as former football coach James Franklin and his recruits.

As for the fest, she said The Corner Room “has never done anything like this” in her time there.

The “Family Fun Zone” will run from 3 to 6 p.m., consisting of an Alpha Fire Company Fire Truck, a carnival carousel, face painting, balloons, art and a live DJ. From 6:30 p.m. to 12:30 a.m., Zeno’s Pub is hosting two local bands, Mick Mac and Americana band Pure Cane Sugar.

The “Centennial Ceremony” from 6 to 7 p.m. includes live music, a moment of “reflections and commemorations,” a Happy Birthday song and a cake presented by Comet Properties, a local real estate company.

Albegiani said she is excited for all of it, and that it’s going to be “a magical evening.” She’ll be able to enjoy all of the festivities right as she’s done working.

“Having a carousel blows my mind,” Albegiani said. “Without the community, we wouldn’t be what we are today.”

John Harris, a local resident, has been going to The Corner Room since 1961.

After a “major tragedy,” Harris considered leaving the borough for New York City. But one Corner Room waitress, Martha Greenway, was especially nice to him during this difficult time, and he said this was central in his decision to make State College his home.

Harris said the restaurant remains a “central force” in the community, and it’s always attracting new faces.

“It’s become a kind of cultural institution in some way or another,” Harris said. “The wait staff there has always been supportive in one way or another.”

With Pat Croce & Company, the place has drifted from being university-oriented, Harris added. Though he said students are essential to the town, the place has shifted its focus toward becoming more of a family establishment, drawing in more people than previously would have come.

Harris said he has never seen the restaurant host an event like Friday’s fest before, and hopes the heat doesn’t impact it in any way.

“This will be unique,” Harris said.

Lily Persio has been serving at The Corner Room for two years, but she said its long history in State College before her arrival is what makes it special.

“I've had so many guests that have come in and told me that they had their first date there, or they worked there at some point when they were in college. It's really awesome,” Persio, a 2025 advertising and public relations graduate, said.

She said she met some of her best friends working there and enjoys being able to say a place that has been in the borough for a century was a part of her college experience.

Though management comes and goes, the people are still the core of the restaurant, she said, and she finds there to be mutual respect between the borough and the restaurant.

“The fest will be a great opportunity for the community to come together,” Persio said.

title: What to know before you visit Arts Fest 2026
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Every July, over 125,000 alumni and visitors flock to State College to experience The Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts. The festival, nicknamed Arts Fest, will run from July 8 to July 12 this year.

Arts Fest offers a variety of creative exhibits, performances and activities and is one of the largest non-football alumni weekends for Penn State. This year marks Arts Fest’s 60th anniversary.

Here’s everything you need to know before you visit Arts Fest 2026.

Events

Arts Fest will begin on Wednesday with a Children & Youth Day Sidewalk Sale on South Allen Street. Young artists from the ages of eight through 18 are encouraged to create “individually crafted, original objects,” the Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts website read.

Afterward, there will be the Children & Youth Day Grand Procession, which begins on Old Main Lawn and proceeds to Sidney Friedman Park.

Other events on Wednesday include a yoga lab, caricatures and puppet shows, family friendly improv and a community sing-along.

On Thursday, the 2026 Sidewalk Sale and Exhibition will kick off. Over 300 independent artists and entrepreneurs will present their wares on the street across 20 categories and a wide range of mediums, including jewelry, ceramics and housewares.

Each exhibitor is visited by the festival’s jurors as part of the jury process, and $20,000 in prize money will be awarded amongst the prize-winning artists. On Saturday, the awards ceremony will take place on the Allen Street Stage.

This year, Arts Fest is debuting a brand-new Live Mural Exhibition to celebrate the country’s 250th anniversary. The juried exhibition, “Dreaming American Futures,” will focus on the overarching theme from “The Hill We Climb” by Amanda Gorman, as well as an excerpt from the book

Five artists have been selected to participate in the inaugural event: Jennifer Lonsky and Ruben Rosado of Pink Sky Designs, Scott Kowalski, Elody Gyekis, Kate Murphy, and Margalo Guo.

A variety of events, concerts and activities are available; the festival’s website includes a daily program with more information.

Festival poster

For over half a century, the poster for Arts Fest has either been designed or influenced by Lanny Sommese, the former head of the Penn State Department of Graphic Design, who passed away in 2022.

From 2023 to 2025, his daughter Saige Sommese carried the torch of tradition, “collaborating” with her father by using his concepts to create finished pieces.

This year, Arts Fests asked Saige to create a poster for the festival that was originally hers. The 36” x 24” depicts a graphic birthday cake with candles representing samples from former festival posters.

The posters are freely available at the following locations until the end of the festival, as listed on the website:

Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts on 403 S. Allen St., Suite 205A

Downtown State College Improvement District on 127 S. Fraser St.

State College Framing Company and Gallery on 160 Rolling Ridge Dr.

The Happy Valley Adventure Bureau on 204 W. Beaver Ave.

Hintz Family Alumni Center on Penn State’s campus

After the festival, the poster will exclusively be available on Art Fest’s online store.

Travel

Arts Fest encourages visitors to use Penn State’s West Parking Deck on White Course Drive, which is located near the IST building.

“Parking is $2 for the first hour, $3 for under two hours and $10 for between two and ten hours, cash and credit cards accepted,” according to Art Fest’s travel guide.

There, visitors can use the free bus service to shuttle to and from the festival. The shuttle goes straight from the West Parking Deck to the festival’s center at the corner of College Avenue and Allen Street.

The bus will make an additional stop at the Palmer Museum of Art on Thursday, Friday and Saturday from 9 a.m. to 11 p.m. and Sunday from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m.

Downtown municipal parking garages are available on Beaver Avenue, Fraser Street and Pugh Street. Hourly rates are $2.25 per hour or “any portion thereof,” and the maximum daily rate is $32.

Art Fest has also made a festival map, which is available on their website to print or download.

Accommodations

This year, Arts Fest has chosen Hyatt Place State College, Scholar Hotel State College and The Penn Stater as their preferred hotel partners. All three are located near campus.

The Penn Stater is offering special rates for Arts Fest guests: $179 per night, with a minimum stay of two nights. Attendees must use the code FEST26 for the specialty rates.

Arts Fest also recommends Wyndham Garden State College, Sleep Inn State College near Penn State, Ramada Hotel and Conference Center, Country Inn & Suites, Holiday Inn Express, Days Inn by Wyndham Penn State and Super 8 by Wyndham State College.

A full list of lodging options can also be found on the Happy Valley Adventure Bureau website.

title: ‘The honor of a lifetime’ | As America turns 250, Penn State athletes reflect on representing their country
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
When Sophia Gladieux was growing up, she looked up to Simone Biles, Michael Jordan and other American athletes, dreaming of the moment she could wear a United States jersey and represent her home country on the field.

A decade later, she earned a spot on the U.S. women’s national field hockey team and competed in the 2024 Paris Olympics. She helped the team to a ninth-place finish in her Olympic debut before returning to Penn State for her final season.

“It’s always an overwhelming sense of pride and joy to be where I am, just representing your country well,” Gladieux told The Daily Collegian. “Going to a school like Penn State, I think I know a little bit about pride and what that means. I had the same feeling wearing a Penn State jersey.”

Although wearing the Team USA jersey never loses its significance, Gladieux said standing on the field before her first Olympic game and singing the national anthem is the moment she remembers the most.

“I get chills thinking about that,” Gladieux said. “It’s really just a full circle moment, to be able to say that you’re part of Team USA.”

Gladieux is just one of several current and former Penn State athletes and coaches reflecting on what it means to represent the country as it approaches its 250th anniversary on Saturday.

Goalkeeper Jenny Rizzo, one of Gladieux’s current U.S. teammates, was drawn to international play by Charlene Morett-Curtiss, her coach at Penn State. Gladieux was also coached by Morett-Curtiss for three years.

Morett-Curtiss was selected to two Olympic teams in 1980 and 1984, taking home bronze in the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics. She played in more than 100 international matches for the U.S., serving as a role model to the Nittany Lion student-athletes.

“The realization that I could follow in her footsteps came when I was in college and her telling stories about her time on the U.S. team,” Rizzo told the Collegian. “Having a coach that makes it tangible for you and encourages you in your dream really instilled a lot of confidence in myself and this dream that I had.”

Years later, Rizzo found herself living that very dream after qualifying for her first Olympic team.

After helping the U.S. clinch its spot in the 2024 Paris Olympics, she stood in a packed, 18,000-seat stadium in India as a light show filled the arena before a match, where the reality of representing the United States finally set in.

“I remember just standing there and being in absolute awe that this is my life,” Rizzo said.

Representing Team USA also gave athletes the opportunity to inspire those hoping to follow in their footsteps. As Gladieux and Rizzo have traveled the world competing on the U.S. women’s field hockey team, they’ve seen a wide range of access to sports for young individuals.

“The cool thing about this day and age is there’s so much opportunity for women in sports in the U.S.,” Rizzo said. “When you look at the history of sport in America, it reminds me that sport is bigger than just putting a ball in the goal. It’s about breaking barriers and giving opportunities for people who are marginalized.”

Now in her fifth year with the national team, Rizzo said she has embraced the opportunity to use her platform to encourage young athletes.

“It’s what our country is all about,” Rizzo said. “No matter where athletes come from, they can represent their country if they want it enough.”

Similarly, Gladieux realized she now occupies the same role Olympians once held for her: Showing younger generations in the field hockey world that it’s possible to achieve their dreams.

For Tessa Janecke, being able to represent the U.S. on the international stage “means everything.”

“To be a part of that legacy, and especially with such a great country … it’s something to be super proud of, so I’m very grateful,” Janecke told the Collegian.

Before finishing one of the most decorated careers in Penn State women’s hockey history, Janecke took her talents to the 2026 Milano Cortina Winter Olympics on Team USA, where she helped bring home the third gold medal in U.S. women’s hockey history. She’s the first Penn State athlete to win gold at the Winter Olympics.

“Putting on the jersey just means you’re about to step into an experience that’s much bigger than yourself,” Janecke said.

While Penn State women’s lacrosse coach Kayla Treanor no longer suits up for Team USA, she remains involved with the program as an assistant coach.

Treanor spent more than 15 years representing the U.S. in some capacity and has watched the sport evolve. Along the way, she became one of the best lacrosse players in U.S. history, currently holding five national records.

Now, she hopes to help send the next wave of Penn State athletes to the national team.

“I’m looking forward to the future and getting our athletes on that roster,” Treanor told the Collegian. “It’s quite the experience, and I do hope that our future Nittany Lions are going to be able to experience that as well.”

While today’s athletes spoke of fulfilling childhood dreams, Treanor now hopes to help future Penn Staters realize their own.

“It’s just about pride in representing your country and I think it’s given me an even stronger tie to the United States,” Treanor said. “It makes me really proud to be a citizen.”

title: 'Keep them on the field' | Resiliency key for Penn State strength coach Reid Kagy
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Reid Kagy was with Iowa State for a total of six years, and he most recently acted as the Cyclones’ director of football strength and conditioning, a role that began in 2023.

That changed when he followed Matt Campbell from Iowa State. Kagy was hired to be the Nittany Lions’ head strength and conditioning coach, replacing Chuck Losey, who followed James Franklin to Virginia Tech.

With his arrival in Happy Valley, Kagy brought a coaching philosophy built on harnessing the capabilities of each individual position group. He wants what he teaches in the weight room to translate directly to the field, and he plans to continue training certain aspects of each group differently depending on specific needs.

“I want to impact play on the field,” Kagy told reporters at Penn State’s annual Lift for Life event. “Power, speed, strength, resiliency, toughness, grit, fortitude — all those things go into that. I want to make sure our guys know what we're doing, why we're doing it, and how it's gonna impact them on the football field.”

Under Losey, Penn State was consistently churning out athletic monsters, and that was a big reason why the Nittany Lions have seen so much success at the NFL draft in recent years.

So it’s up to Kagy to continue that legacy. And his philosophy is doing so reflects plenty of the other coaches that followed Campbell from the Cyclones.

“We're trying to get faster, we're trying to get stronger, we're trying to be more powerful, we're trying to have the ability to repeat efforts,” Kagy said. “We're trying to be disciplined, tough, physical on the field — most violent team in the nation is what we would love to be.”

Team bonding was another point of emphasis for Kagy. He understood coming into this position that making connections between two worlds — Iowa State and Penn State — would be imperative to the team’s success on the field.

Kagy noted that the purpose of winter and summer camps is for building said relationships between the players but also between the players and their coaches. Kagy has been learning each player’s personality to know how to coach them in the most efficient way, citing how he can “push the right buttons at the right time.”

Kagy’s philosophy mirrors that of Campbell’s, as the team’s bond is something that Penn State’s new head coach stressed over the course of the offseason. Campbell reasons that the bond forged between teammates is a crucial aspect of his players’ ability to “fight through adversity.”

“We want to create relationships,” Kagy said. “We want to be a part of that, bridging the gap and making sure this team is together. The most together team is usually the most successful team. We're building that right now.”

The strength and conditioning staff spends eight hours a week with the players, and since time is limited, Kagy is working to ensure that every minute spent training is meaningful.

“If there's something in the program that's either not dialed in towards (impacting play or building relationships), it's probably a waste of our time,” Kagy said. “We don't have endless time with our guys, so we got to focus on the big rocks. We're going to make an impact on the field and keep them on the field.”

title: 2028 QB James Armstrong commits to Penn State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has found its quarterback of the future from Pennsylvania.

The Nittany Lions earned a commitment from James Armstrong on Thursday.

Armstrong, a 4-star, is the No. 10 quarterback in his class and No. 4 player from Pennsylvania, per 247Sports composite. He is Penn State's third commitment in the 2028 class with cornerback Deonte Flemings Jr. and kicker Carter Petri.

Armstrong visited Happy Valley for camp on June 10, participating in drills with quarterbacks coach Jake Waters and other campers.

The Aliquippa native wrapped up his sophomore season with 21 touchdowns against three interceptions. Armstrong threw for 2,232 yards and rushed for another 799 yards and 16 touchdowns.

Armstrong joins 2027 commit Will Wood as future quarterbacks for the Nittany Lions.

title: Potential Penn State offensive breakouts in 2026
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
From a new quarterback to a new playcaller, Penn State’s offense will look very different this season.

While there are several players who are expected to lead the charge in this new era for Penn State, there’s always some unexpected players who end up making a big difference.

Here’s a look at some potential offensive breakout candidates under Year 1 with Matt Campbell.

Koby Howard

One of the most expected breakouts in 2026 is wide receiver Koby Howard, who made a handful of starts in his true freshman season last year. Now in Year 2, Howard is likely to be one of the three starting receivers alongside Iowa State transfers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen.

The Florida native recorded just seven catches during his first year, but averaged 19 yards per catch with his longest reception going for 31 yards.

With veteran receivers in Sowell and Eskildsen in the mix — both who played with quarterback Rocco Becht at Iowa State — Howard will likely be favored less than the two familiar faces. However, he caught the eye of Campbell early and is expected to take off in his sophomore season.

“Koby is a young, talented football player,” Campbell said in April. “He’s got great potential and we’re really grateful to have him back.”

Quinton Martin Jr.

Penn State’s running back room will undoubtedly look much different without stars Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen. The exact depth chart is to be determined with Martin and transfers James Peoples and Carson Hansen, but the returning Nittany Lion is expected to make a much bigger contribution this season.

Last season, Martin’s sole contribution came in the Pinstripe Bowl against Clemson, where he recorded 103 rushing yards on 20 attempts, including a career-best 21-yard rush.

During the offseason, the Pennsylvania native has worked with new running backs coach Savon Huggins to improve his fitness and skillset, as well as adding some weight to his now 212-pound frame.

“I think I’ve taken a big step forward in (being a) running back all-around, just getting more downhill, pad-level, producing and just staying consistent,” Martin said in April.

Malachi Goodman

Former 4-star recruit and No. 9 offensive tackle in his class, Malachi Goodman did not see the field in his first year at Penn State. Now, he’s expected to be the starting left tackle as a redshirt freshman.

Both Goodman and his position group as a whole have drawn a lot of praise from both Ryan Clanton and Campbell, who’ve called the offensive tackles one of the best positions on the field.

“Boy, I don’t know if I could be any more impressed with what I’ve seen from that group,” Campbell said in April. “Goodman’s 330 (pounds) and he looks like he’s 250 (pounds). He’s smooth. He may be a redshirt freshman, but I feel like his maturity of physical ability is way beyond that.”

Cooper Cousins

Entering Penn State, there was high praise and potential for Cooper Cousins, who was selected as a true freshman preseason All-American by 247Sports. While he never got to establish himself, making just two starts through two seasons, he showed flashes of success.

Cousins will without a doubt take on a starting role on Penn State’s offensive line this season, likely at right guard, and will be able to cement those successful moments as a starter.

Despite the new faces along the offensive line, Cousins has emerged as a leader. The junior took the opportunity to play a variety of positions at Blue-White Practice, showcasing his abilities on the field.

“(Clanton) asked me and really all the offensive line, what he wanted to do and get better at this spring,” Cousins said after Blue-White Practice. “Me personally, I wanted to play all the positions so I can learn all of them. That way I know what my tackles are thinking, what my centers are thinking, and all that.”

Andrew Rappleyea

After playing in just three games with no stats in his first two seasons — including a season-ending injury his redshirt freshman season — tight end Andrew Rappleyea made a big jump in 2025.

Rappleyea earned eight starts and appeared in another five games, recording 20 receptions for 180 yards and three touchdowns during his redshirt sophomore season.

Despite the increased playing time, Rappleyea was still considered the No. 3 tight end on the team behind Khalil Dinkins and Luke Reynolds. This year, he’s expected to take the second spot behind Iowa State transfer Benjamin Brahmer.

title: How the One Big Beautiful Bill Act changes student loans
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Student loan provisions from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) began on Wednesday, following a final rule by the U.S. Department of Education on May 1 to implement most of the law.

The OBBBA was signed into law on July 4, 2025 by President Donald Trump. Undergraduate, graduate and professional students can all expect changes to loan limits and repayment plans.

Here’s everything you need to know about student loans under the OBBBA.

Lifetime Limits

All new undergraduate, graduate and professional student borrowers have a lifetime maximum loan limit of $257,500. This includes all subsidized and unsubsidized loans received for undergraduate studies, graduate and professional studies, as well as PLUS loans received prior to entering graduate or professional studies.

“Even if you pay off or receive a discharge on any amount of your loans, you can’t borrow more than the lifetime maximum loan limit,” a statement on the Federal Student Aid’s website read.

However, full-time students and part-time students will have different annual loan limits. Part-time students’ annual loan limits will be adjusted based on enrollment level.

New Undergraduate Borrowers

There are a few guidelines under OBBBA for new undergraduate students expecting loan disbursements starting on or after Wednesday.

Loan Limits

While annual and aggregate loan limits will not change, academic institutions may set a loan limit lower than the legal maximum. OBBBA allows this as long as all borrowers in the same program of study are given the same limit.

Direct PLUS Loans

Parents who receive Direct PLUS Loans will now have reduced borrowing eligibility. Dependent undergraduates can receive up to $20,000 per year from their parents, and the overall aggregate limit of $65,000 includes “any amount paid in full or discharged,” according to the Federal Student Aid statement.

Parents reaching the annual or aggregate limit for PLUS loans prevents them from receiving additional PLUS Loans and doesn’t increase their dependent’s unsubsidized loan limit.

If one or more parents have adverse credit and cannot receive a PLUS loan, their dependent’s annual Direct Unsubsidized Loan limit will increase.

New Graduate Student Borrowers

Loan Limits

Academic institutions may set a loan limit lower than the legal maximum for graduate students, but all borrowers in the same program of study must be given the same limit.

While loan limits for graduate and professional students were previously identical, graduate students will now have different limits from students enrolled in programs of study leading to professional degrees.

Graduate students will have a reduced aggregate loan limit compared to those active before Wednesday. Those enrolled in graduate programs may receive unsubsidized loans up to $20,500 per year and up to $100,000 in total if they have never been professional students.

“This aggregate loan limit won’t include loans received for undergraduate study, but includes all Direct Subsidized and Unsubsidized Loans made for graduate study,” according to the Federal Office of Student Aid.

For students who were professional students but are now graduate students, unsubsidized loans of up to $200,000 (minus disbursements for their professional program) are available. This figure includes all subsidized and unsubsidized loans for graduate or professional study.

Direct PLUS Loans are no longer available for graduate students.

New Professional Student Borrowers

Professional degrees are typically doctoral level degrees requiring “at least six academic years of postsecondary education to complete, including at least two years of post-baccalaureate coursework, as well as generally requiring professional licensure to begin practice in the intended field,” according to the Federal Student Aid website.

Professional degrees, as defined by the Federal Aid Office, fall into the following programs: Pharmacy (Pharm.D.); Dentistry (D.D.S. or D.M.D.); Veterinary Medicine (D.V.M.); Chiropractic (DC or DCM); Law (L.L.B. or J.D.); Medicine (M.D.); Optometry (O.D.); Osteopathic Medicine (D.O.); Podiatry (D.P.M, D.P. or Pod.D.); Theology (M.Div. or M.H.L.); and Clinical Psychology (Psy.D. or Ph.D.).

Loan Limits

Professional degree seekers will receive loan limits that differ from those given to graduate students. They will be eligible for up to $50,000 in unsubsidized annual loans.

Annual and aggregate loan limits for professional students have increased in comparison to pre-July figures, according to the Federal Student Aid statement.

If professional students were not previously enrolled in graduate programs, they may receive unsubsidized loans up to $200,000. If they were previously enrolled in graduate programs, they may receive unsubsidized loans up to $200,000 minus any disbursement received for graduate school.

Direct PLUS Loans are no longer available for professional students.

Repayment Plans for Undergraduate, Graduate and Professional Students

Students whose loans are disbursed from July 1 onwards must repay all eligible direct loans under the Repayment Assistance Plan (RAP) or the Tiered Standard Plan.

Students with direct subsidized loans, direct unsubsidized loans, direct PLUS loans for graduate or professional students or direct consolidation loans that do not include parent PLUS loans are allowed to repay their loans with either RAP or the Tiered Standard Plan.

Parent PLUS loans or direct consolidation loans including parent PLUS loans may only be repaid under the Tiered Standard Plan.

title: Off the record | The hard truth
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Dear reader,

Wow. Wow. Wow. This is a lot to take in.

This, to me, feels like the ultimate betrayal. You confide in someone about a personal aspect of your life, and then to have them engage in sexual activity with that very person is insane.

I understand why this has been on your mind for years. But I do believe that the longer you let this weigh on your conscience, the longer she’s “winning.” I can guarantee she hasn’t thought about her actions in years, while you let it have a direct effect on the way you view yourself and the others in your life.

Allow yourself to recognize that in the moment, you felt comfortable enough with her to tell your thoughts, and you would never have expected her to do what she did. You could never have predicted that your close friend would act this way, and it reflects on her character, not your vulnerability. Of course you told your friend about your crush. That’s what people do.

I’ve told my friends things I probably should’ve taken to my grave, but I would never expect them to exploit that information to their own benefit, let alone something so private and intimate. She shouldn’t have capitalized on your confidential information, plain and simple.

I know you think you won’t ever be truly over it, but you can begin a process of recovering your sense of self and trust in others.

She doesn’t deserve forgiveness. She knew exactly how you felt and did it anyway. Instead, you have to forgive yourself.

The first step is understanding that you did nothing wrong. You can’t let this experience be the reason you never open up. People can be mean and deceitful, but that doesn’t mean you shouldn’t try again. You’re only doing yourself a disservice by letting her stunt your growth in relationships.

I can’t help but feel like you may have been in relationships, romantic or otherwise, that have been tainted by this experience, and, to be honest, it’s entirely in your hands to change the narrative.

She’s not going to apologize, so it’s on you to rewire your way of thinking. It’s not fair for those in your life to have to prove that they're not going to betray you. The jeep incident is the outlier and not the standard for the way people choose to behave, especially if they treasure your company.

As you enter new relationships, the only person who can decide that enough is enough is you. No more wallowing in feelings of doubt, everyone is a fresh start, so treat it as such.

Maybe try approaching new friendships with an “innocent until proven guilty” mindset. Don’t assume they have bad intentions unless they do something that says otherwise. This way, you have a healthy level of skepticism, but you aren’t letting your past sabotage the future.

Outside of relationships, the love you have for yourself has to come from within. If your perception of self is based on what your friends think of you, stop it. People are your friends because they love and appreciate the time spent with the authentic, original you.

When you're down on yourself, go on a walk, watch a movie, eat your guilty pleasure snack, do something that reminds you that you're the prize. Talk to the people in your life who remind you there’s still good in people.

No one is entitled to your friendship, and you are entitled to be selective about who you let into your life and share things with.

It’s okay to have some short walls up, those can always be scaled. And isn’t part of the American dream a white-picket fence?

Just don’t build them so high that they’re impossible to knock down.

I sympathize with you and the heartbreak that friendship betrayal can bring, but take pride in knowing you’ll never let yourself feel like that again, because now you know better.

Until next time,

Miss Behave

title: Could Beaver Stadium ever host the World Cup?
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
The knockout rounds of the 2026 World Cup are in full swing, and stadiums across North America are filled with soccer fans from all regions of the globe.

Major cities in the United States, Canada and Mexico have seen a booming increase in tourism for one of the world’s grandest sporting events.

Yet none of the college football stadiums are being used in official World Cup matches despite some having much larger capacities. This includes Penn State’s Beaver Stadium — the current second-largest stadium in North America, pending renovations.

There are many factors to look into when considering Beaver Stadium as a potential host, should the United States place a bid to host in 2038, and it’s a complicated process that requires the stadium and the surrounding area to fit a specific criteria.

Let’s take a deeper look at whether or not Happy Valley fits that bill.

Stadium capacity, renovations

Beaver Stadium could host a World Cup game based on sheer size alone. Coming in at a capacity of over 106,000 spectators, the venue dwarfs the vast majority of the current lineup.

AT&T Stadium has approximately 94,000 seats and is hosting the most games: Nine, including one of the semifinals. Games like that, potentially featuring world-class superstars such as Lionel Messi, Christiano Ronaldo or Kylian Mbappé, are typically highly coveted experiences where tickets sell out in an instant.

Being able to fit an extra 10,000 spectators would be a major opportunity for FIFA to maximize revenue.

On top of that, FIFA would have the chance to set the U.S. single-game attendance record for a soccer game. In 2014, 109,318 spectators filled Michigan Stadium for a friendly between Manchester United and Real Madrid, but Penn State has already beaten that figure for football games without international soccer fans, which are known as some of the most passionate in the world.

The Nittany Lion faithful regularly surpass that figure for the biggest games of the season such as the annual White Out. After renovating the west-side seating, Vice President of Intercollegiate Athletics Pat Kraft said the stadium could reach “north of 108,000” seats according to a report by the Centre Daily Times.

In 2024, Penn State’s game against Ohio State set the school’s attendance record at 111,030.

Additionally, by the time the United States has the chance to host another World Cup, the Beaver Stadium revitalization project will have been completed. That means international fans would be getting an improved experience compared to what it would’ve been this past time around.

The playing surface, pitch size

In order for FIFA to give Beaver Stadium the green light, the field dimensions would have to meet the regulation size. Officially, the standard is set at 105 meters in length by 68 meters in width.

Beaver Stadium, which follows the standardized guidelines set by the NCAA, fits FIFA’s requirement of a 109.7-meter length. However, the surface’s width falls well short, coming in at a mere 48.8 meters from sideline to sideline.

While the field could be expanded, it wouldn’t leave enough space for team benches or the roughly five meters of runoff space required for player safety.

For it to work, Penn State would have to permanently reconstruct the stadium to remove parts of the lower bowl, as seen with Lincoln Financial Field in Philadelphia. The home of the Philadelphia Eagles had to demolish the concrete sections closest to the playing field, and replace them with mobile structures for ease of transition between gridiron football and soccer.

Considering that Beaver Stadium will have already undergone a massive $700 million renovation, it’s unlikely that the university would jump at the chance for another costly investment.

Tourism, the location problem

Regardless of whether Beaver Stadium renovates the stadium to FIFA’s regulation size, there’s the matter of location.

State College is in the middle of nowhere. A veritable diamond in the rough, the quaint college town is roughly three hours west of Philadelphia and three hours east of Pittsburgh — two cities that have more to do and more to see in terms of tourism.

As we’ve seen with the Scots in Boston, the bars get flooded at night, and there simply aren’t enough of them in State College to accommodate more than 100,000 thirsty fans.

Tourists want to experience the urban United States in the time between soccer games. Being able to see landmarks like the Liberty Bell, Statue of Liberty or the White House is simply more desirable to the average traveler.

The town would also have to consider where tourists would stay. As Penn State is farther away from major cities, there’s a lack of hotels. So the fans would likely have to book hotels in Harrisburg or Altoona, meaning FIFA would have to organize mass public transport options. That could be more hassle than it’s worth.

While Beaver Stadium’s capacity is attractive to FIFA and fans alike, there are far too many logistical concerns for Penn State to deal with in order to turn that thought into action.

title: Column | Rating Penn State’s new Adidas merchandise
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s 33-year Nike era ended on Wednesday, as the university launched Adidas as its next official sponsor for all athletic wear and merchandise.

As a result of the change, Penn State Athletics released the new uniforms and merchandise under the new partnership.

For weeks, the media and fans have been speculating over what the Adidas era would look like, including talks about Penn State’s original pink and black colors. While there wasn’t a drastic change from the previous Nike apparel, here’s how I grade the new release of merchandise.

Football jersey: 9/10

The new jersey design is pretty much identical to Penn State’s under Nike, the only difference being the three stripes instead of the swoosh. That’s a good thing, though, as the Nittany Lions are known for their simple basic blues.

Primary t-shirt: 8/10

Once again, not much has changed from the Nike branded clothing. The color is the same and the logo is the same, but the Adidas logo centered on the shirt — which seems like the norm for a majority of the apparel — takes away from the Penn State theme a little bit.

Wordmark t-shirt: 5/10

This is arguably my least favorite piece of merchandise from the entire adidas collection. As someone commented on a similar-looking shirt, “Are they members of Adidas or Penn State?”

Solid navy polo: 9/10

There’s nothing to dislike about this polo shirt. It follows the same standard sports-polo theme with the school logo on the left chest and company logo on the right.

Primary hoodie: 9/10

The hoodie falls in line with the rest of the new merchandise, with the Adidas logo sitting above the Nittany Lion head. Fortunately, the Penn State logo is much larger than the Adidas logo on this item.

Football t-shirt: 7/10

I like the creativity on the football t-shirt, but I think there’s still room to grow on the end product. Everything being stacked on top of one another inside the box just seems off.

Happy Valley blend t-shirt: 10/10

This is easily my favorite shirt in the entire collection. Not only is it a new design as opposed to a recycled previous design with the Adidas logo, but it fills up the shirt and has a lot of creativity.

It also brings back some retro elements, something Adidas seems to want to do more of in the future.

Sleeveless hoodie: 9/10

One look at this and it screams Ryan Clanton. The Penn State logo looks quite small in the top corner, but overall looks clean against the white and light grey adidas logo.

Quarterzip pullover: 6/10

The cartoon depiction of the Nittany Lion is certainly a choice for the pullover. There’s little resemblance to the Nittany Lion mascot, it’s the wrong color navy and it doesn’t even say “PSU.”

Black polo: 10/10

I wouldn’t be surprised if Matt Campbell were to sport this polo or something similar on gameday. After all, he frequently wore all-black attire at Iowa State.

The black polo offers a clean-looking, secondary alternative to the primary Penn State colors.

title: ‘This is not happening in a vacuum’ | Penn State expands AI initiatives with new studio, literacy course
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Correction: A previous version of this article stated the AI Studio would be funded entirely from the budget of the office of the provost with no contributions from sources, such as the University Park Allocation Committee, also known as UPAC; tuition and student fees. The Office of the Provost's budget is funded primarily by tuition and state appropriations.

For months, Penn State has been expanding its artificial intelligence (AI) initiatives. That now includes AI Studio and an AI literacy course.

According to Penn State’s AI website, AI Studio currently provides students, faculty and staff with access to OpenAI’s ChatGPT models 4.1 and 5.4, Anthropic’s Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Haiku 4.5 and Google Gemini models 3 flash and 3.1 pro — all paid versions.

AI Essentials at Penn State, released alongside AI Studio, is a self-guided course on the university’s AI literacy policies.

The tools are available for students enrolled in summer classes, and will be officially rolled out for everyone in the fall semester, according to a Penn State press release. All students, faculty and staff on every campus will have access to both the studio and course.

Executive Vice President and Provost Fotis Sotiropoulus has been on the forefront of the university’s AI Transformation initiative. AI research at Penn State has been increasing, and in May, Vasant Honavar was named Penn State’s first-ever vice provost for artificial intelligence.

The university’s guidelines for using artificial intelligence require students to check their course syllabus or request permission from their instructors before using an AI tool for assignments, tests and quizzes, according to the AI site.

Students are also responsible to check their AI usage for “bias, discrimination and other ethical issues” and ensure generated content is “accurate, reliable, and contains appropriate citations to real sources.”

Students, faculty and staff are also advised not to put in any level 3 or level 4 data — sensitive information that can be found in documents such as a driver’s license or medical records — into an AI tool to prevent it from being used to train the AI model.

Why it’s using paid models

David Gindhart, associate vice president of Information Technology and co-chair of the AI Coordinating Council, said the decision was partly influenced by student feedback on AI usage during the fall 2025 semester.

Some students reported paying for tools, though most were using free versions. Additionally, students reported having multiple accounts to override usage limitations.

“It became pretty obvious that there was this inequity within a classroom, where you could have two students sitting next to each other, and one's got access to robust tools and doing assignments with them, and others are using free tools,” Gindhart said.

Additionally, Gindhart said paid models prevent AI Studio user data from being used to train current and future versions of AI, which is more likely to occur when using free-access models.

AI Studio is powered by software associated with Cloudforce’s NebulaONE AI gateway. Penn State signed a contract with the company earlier this year to configure NebulaONE to run within Penn State’s Microsoft Azure environment and be protected under Penn State’s existing data protection agreement with Microsoft, according to Gindhart.

“Cloudforce develops the AI Studio software but does not host or store any Penn State data, so a breach of Cloudforce’s systems would not expose user data. Access is secured by Penn State’s standard identity controls, including multi-factor authentication,” he said.

According to the AI site, automated tools within AI Studio “may analyze anonymized content to identify common themes and usage patterns … to support reliability and continuous improvement.”

Chat logs within AI Studio are also expected to be retained for one year to comply with Penn State’s general records retention policy and AD35 University Archives and Records Management.

Gindhart said this would provide students with better privacy when using AI Studio and prevent “university-unique” materials and activities from being leaked through the AI models.

Paid versions operate because of AI tokens, small units of data that AI models process when generating responses to users.

Many paid AI models charge for every 1 million tokens used for making and storing input and for generating output. Input tokens are often less expensive than output tokens as the latter require more processing power.

But the inability to calculate with certainty the amount of tokens needed to run these processes at any given time, and on any given model, makes it difficult for companies to keep AI usage costs low.

The data provider AlphaStreet estimated roughly 300 companies addressed questions or concerns about AI tokens in April or May during earnings calls or public discussions with financial analysts, according to an article by Wired.

The outlet also referenced efforts by some companies, like Meta and Uber, to introduce caps for their employees’ AI usage in response to accumulated costs from previously unrestricted AI token spending.

In April, New York-based clothing brand Baseball Lifestyle 101 told about 50 of its top managers to spend the equivalent of about 20 percent of their salary on AI tokens every month, Wired wrote.

The average annual salary of a Baseball Lifestyle 101 store manager is $58,923, according to Indeed. 20 percent of that is $11,784.60. Baseball Lifestyle 101 co-founder and Chief Strategy Officer Bill Rom told Wired the cost of using AI tokens for models such as Claude was likely to exceed $100,000 a month by the end of 2026.

Gindhart said all AI tokens from AI Studio would be funded entirely from the budget of the office of the Provost with no contributions from other sources, such as the University Park Allocation Committee, also known as UPAC and student fees will not contribute to financing AI Studio.

AI Studio will allot users limited AI tokens, Gindhart said, to help people learn how to budget them effectively.

“These tokens cost different amounts of money, depending on which model you're using,” Gindhart said. “By having that cap, you're actually having to make some of those decisions yourself about, ‘How much work do I have to do?’ and ‘What's the most cost effective way to do that?’”

Faculty see room for improvement

Rifat Mansur, an assistant teaching professor of Information Science and Technology, is currently teaching an A-I 100 course at Penn State Lehigh Valley. Mansur attended sessions featuring AI Studio during Penn State Harrisburg’s AI and Teaching Summit.

He said while he appreciates the selection of AI models being offered, Mansur said some of the flaws he encountered from his personal use of AI, such as in Sonnet 4.6, could hinder how people are able to use AI Studio.

“Sonnet 4.6 is something that I used to use a lot but it has a problem of getting too verbose. They keep on going and it will take a lot of tokens,” Mansur said. “If you say, ‘write an article in, say, 500 words,’ it will go for 1,000 words.”

Mansur prefers Opus models 4.6 and 4.8 — a different branch of Claude code — for personal tasks and teaching. He also found Sonnet 4.5 to be more flexible than its successor in carrying out minor tasks.

Similarly, he said people favoring a version of AI not supported by AI Studio might decrease usage of the platform. However, Mansur said exposure to the models offered by AI Studio, like in class assignments, could offset that.

David Callejo Pérez, chancellor of Penn State Harrisburg, said AI Studio democratizes access to a wide array of AI models without individual Commonwealth campuses worrying about purchasing the licenses themselves.

He said it’s imperative for Penn State to account for tools like AI Studio when thinking about higher education.

“Our job is not to stop you from the tool. Our job is to integrate the tool into teaching. In order for me to know that you're learning, I got to figure out a way to judge your ability to learn,” Callejo Pérez said.

Honavar said use of AI Studio will not be enforced by the university. It is up to the discretion of both faculty and students to use AI Studio based on their preferences — though some “broad guidelines” might be introduced.

Beyond that, “what happens in the classroom is really up to the instructor.”

“That's kind of how all courses are run at any university,” Honavar said. “What is appropriate use and what's not is determined by the instructor and, in that context, if a student wants to opt out of it, that's a conversation with the instructor as well.”

Honavar said Penn State is planning to review any “prohibited use” of AI Studio to previously encountered AI tools.

“When an instructor believes an AI tool has been used in a way that is not permitted under course or assessment instructions, the matter is reviewed through the established academic integrity process,” Honavar said.

AI literacy resources

Crystal Ramsay, assistant vice provost for the AI Center of Excellence for Teaching and Learning and a co-chair of the AI Coordinating Council, said like AI Studio, the course is set to be available on all students’ Canvas pages for the fall semester. It is currently available for those enrolled in summer courses.

“You can find various forms of literacy training in lots of places, but we wanted to brand something Penn State and make it thoughtfully created with our faculty, our staff and our students — the entire community — in mind,” Ramsay said.

He said it’s important that other universities also work to better their AI education resources, so students are able to make better choices about AI knowing its benefits and risks. Penn State plans to set up a “student advisory group” to better gauge student feedback as AI resources and education continue expanding.

“All of this is not happening in a vacuum,” Honavar said. “We'll be responsible if we don't provide our students with the literacy and the skills that are needed to thrive in a world that's changing. And every individual will have to make a choice about how they're going to use AI tools — if they're going to use AI tools — in their lives.”

title: Penn State officially releases new merchandise with Adidas
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A new era of Penn State is here.

On July 1, Penn State moved into its 10-year deal with Adidas, ending a decades-long partnership with Nike.

Adidas released a video Wednesday morning of the Nittany Lion mascot putting on a signature Adidas' track suit and a pair of black adidas sneakers.

title: Penn State’s switch to Adidas marks ‘new growth in the sports world,’ marketing experts say
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State wrestling lifted its fifth straight national championship trophy, women’s volleyball won its eighth national title and football came back from a 28-7 deficit to win the 2016 Big Ten Championship in the iconic Nike swoosh.

But after 33 years, the Nittany Lions will be repping a new logo — Adidas’ three stripes.

In September, Penn State announced a $300 million, 10-year deal with Adidas. Beginning Wednesday, the brand will serve as the university’s official athletic department-wide footwear, uniform, apparel and sideline partner.

Sports marketing experts say the deal, at its core, is about more than a simple logo change. It reflects the business-orientated approach to college sports, with apparel companies competing for proximity to the marquee brands.

“Supposedly, it'll make things stronger, better, more effective, more successful on both sides, treating the athlete now as quasi-professional,” Mark Conrad, director of the sports business concentration at Fordham University, told The Daily Collegian. “In addition, utilizing that as part of the Penn State brand to attract athletes by potentially giving them some money in their pocket.”

A partnership of this magnitude adds a layer of financial pressure to smaller programs, as Adidas is giving the Nittany Lions a hefty boost in Name, Image and Likeness (NIL), considerations for the nation’s top recruits.

“What you're going to see is basically a ‘haves and have nots’ situation, meaning that the powerhouse schools could very well go from strength to strength,” Conrad said. “The mid-major type schools are going to have a more challenging situation, because they don't have the kind of money, arguably, to pay. They don't have necessarily that kind of alumni support, and they’re not going to have the size to necessarily play in that environment.”

Penn State’s success on the national level and long list of traditions make its brand attractive, and the program has a very passionate fanbase with one of the largest alumni networks worldwide. Adidas stands to gain a lot of revenue from the popularity of the Nittany Lion brand.

“(Nike and Adidas) are competing for positioning, they’re competing for brand equity, so Adidas isn't really buying logo placement,” Brett Christenson, the director of Smeal Sports Business Programs at Penn State, said. “This is a brand alignment — proximity to high-quality sports brands. Adidas is that, and Penn State is that.”

Nike’s failure to treat Penn State as a priority was a major factor contributing to the change, according to reporting from the Centre Daily Times. The outlet also reported Nike had the opportunity to match Adidas' offer, but elected to withdraw.

Penn State was one of four schools to originally partner with Nike for athletic department-wide deals alongside North Carolina, Miami and USC in 1993.

Nike’s co-founder Phil Knight and former Nittany Lions coach Joe Paterno forged a deep friendship that began at Nike-related business vacations. The relationship continued up until Paterno’s death in 2011, and Knight delivered the eulogy at his funeral.

Knight graduated from Oregon in 1959 and was a member of the track and field team where he ran under coach Bill Bowerman before the pair co-founded Nike.

“No one’s going to supplant Oregon as being favored by Nike as a company as long as Phil Knight is alive,” Rick Burton, the David B. Falk Emeritus professor of sport management at Syracuse University, said. “Penn State has always been a Nike school, and this is an abrupt switch, which may be a reflection of schools trying to keep up with whoever they think has got an advantage.”

But Penn State isn’t immediately jumping into a position where dominance is guaranteed, despite the expectation of being Adidas' priority. On the football field, programs like Indiana are actively making a case to push for greater heights.

Other notable programs currently partnered with Adidas include Washington, Texas A&M and Miami. Tennessee will join Penn State in switching from the swoosh to the three stripes on Wednesday. Competition on the field will be paramount to the continued success of the deal long-term, regardless of the initial contract’s financial worth, according to Burton.

“Winning solves all problems,” Burton said. “The consumer likes to be associated with a winner. Let Penn State go out and prove it on the field, and they’ll reap the rewards with their marketing partners.”

Former Penn State safety Malik Golden who was with the team from 2012-16 , like many other athletes at the time, grew up wearing Nike. Golden said he assumed Nike and Penn State would maintain a lifetime partnership, because “that’s what everyone kind of figured.”

“(Nike) was the pinnacle of athletic wear,” Golden said. “So, when I got to Penn State, and it was one of the top Nike schools at the time, you get excited about all the new stuff.”

In his playing days, Nike was the undisputed premier apparel brand in college football. The company’s subsidiary, Jordan, launched uniforms featuring the “Jumpman” logo for Michigan in 2016.

But Golden believes the gap between these companies is shrinking, calling recent developments “an arms race to see who can really set themselves apart.”

This isn’t the only time a Penn State tradition has changed in recent years. Penn State Athletics sold West Shore Home naming rights to Beaver Stadium last March, a deal made as the stadium undergoes a $700 million revitalization.

"With the NIL and the changing of the landscape in college sports, they're just more brand conscious, especially in high school, and more worried about life outside of the sport,” Golden said. “It helps you when you get to the NFL, or whatever professional endeavor you're trying to get into.”

The NIL is the dominant force behind recruiting, particularly in football, and there’s a newfound emphasis on the individual player. That’s where the new deal is strongest.

“There is a media component that is unavoidable,” Christenson said. “That's part of a new growth in the sports world, which is that sponsors are going to help support the student-athlete. Not just by clothing them in this case, or giving them performance apparel, they're also going to help build their brand as well. We wouldn't have seen that 10 years ago.”

Penn State Athletics will usher in the new era with an exclusive merchandise sale at Pegula Ice Arena on Wednesday and Thursday from 9 a.m. to 7 p.m. The pop up sale series will also continue from July 8 to 12, and fans can enter through Gate A at the arena to access the Mount Nittany Room where the sale is occurring.

author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's in need of a new leader.

Former head coach Sarah Brown was announced the next head coach at Texas State on Tuesday, ending her eight-year career at Penn State.

title: SEE IT: Penn State releases video of view from seats on renovated west side of Beaver Stadium
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Fans on Beaver Stadium's west side will have a birds eye view of the action.

On Tuesday, Beaver Stadium released a video on X showcasing the view from the seats on the upper west side of the stadium.

Athletic Director Pat Kraft stated that the 2026 seating capacity for the stadium will be "north of 108,000," according to a report by the Centre Daily Times, and could very well be more.

title: Penn State men's basketball to play St. Bonaventure for preseason exhibition in Buffalo
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State will have an exhibition game against a mid-major this upcoming season.

St. Bonaventure will host the Nittany Lions in the Greenlight Network Preseason Showcase at the KeyBank Center in Buffalo on Oct. 17, according to a news release.

The Penn State-St. Bonaventure game will be the second game of a doubleheader that also features Daemen University and St. John Fisher University. Tipoff times, ticket information and broadcast information will all be announced at a later date.

As of now, this is the only announced game that Penn State will have in the preseason. The Nittany Lions had two exhibition games last season against Dayton and Shippensburg University.

Penn State and St. Bonaventure used to be conference opponents when the Nittany Lions were in the Atlantic 10 for men's basketball before joining the Big Ten after the 1990-91 season. The two teams haven't met since they were in the same conference, and the Bonnies hold a 11-10 advantage in the all-time series.

The Nittany Lions have a former St. Bonaventure player on the roster in transfer addition Desonte Bowen, who spent the last two seasons with the Bonnies. St. Bonaventure also added Penn State transfer Mason Blackwood this offseason.

This upcoming exhibition game is being played in Buffalo because the Bonnies, who are from New York, want to build on their "strong presence" in the Buffalo market, according to the news release. Greenlight Networks is a partner with St. Bonaventure Athletics and is helping make this event happen.

title: Film review | 3-star CB Dhillon McGee offers gap control, ferocity to Penn State’s defensive backfield
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State beat Texas and SMU for 3-star cornerback Dhillon McGee out of DeSoto, Texas when he announced his commitment to the Nittany Lions on Wednesday, becoming the third cornerback commit in the Class of 2027.

The 6-foot-0.5, 175-pounder is ranked as the No. 67 player out of Texas and the No. 47 cornerback in the 2027 class, per 247Sports composite. He took his official visit to Happy Valley on June 12.

In addition to playing both cornerback and wide receiver, McGee is a multi-sport athlete at DeSoto High School, playing for the school’s baseball team and running track.

On the field, he finished the 2025 season with 32 tackles, 15 pass breakups and one tackle for loss. He aided the Fighting Eagles to a 55-27 victory over C.E. King High School in the UIL 6A Division II State Championship game at AT&T Stadium.

Here’s a look at McGee’s film.

Controlling the gap

McGee expertly times his pivots when transitioning from backpedaling to running forward, ensuring that he maintains only a half-step distance behind the matched receiver. That way, when the pass is airborne, McGee doesn’t let the receiver get the separation he needs.

On man-to-man press coverage, he shows off his acceleration and long speed to keep up with the receiver’s downhill momentum. This is where his 4.4-second 40-yard dash comes into play — the initial burst of speed marks him as a threat for the entire length of the play.

He’s an incredible athlete with a blend of speed and verticality that allows him to get high and break up lob passes. With experience as a receiver, he’s used to fighting for jump balls on the offensive side of the field, so he’s able to bring that to the defense and potentially cause interceptions.

McGee’s first instinct is to have his hands on the hips and under the arms of his opponent. He loves to play rough, knocking the receiver to the wayside, but still times the attacks perfectly when the ball is descending.

McGee does this best on the sidelines where his opponents don’t have the space to break free.

Open-field physicality, tackling

McGee already possesses the height required of a collegiate cornerback, being on the taller side among Terry Smith’s current room. However, Penn State may look to have him add some muscle to give him an additional surge of power.

When the opposing team runs a screen pass or run and he breaks away from his one-on-one man coverage, McGee attacks the ball carrier with tenacity, throwing his shoulder into the opponent’s ribs and chest.

Although he doesn’t get the player on the ground, he stuns him long enough for backup to arrive. McGee and his teammates overwhelm the carrier and pile on until the whistle is blown.

Along the sidelines, McGee doesn’t need assistance. He’s proficient in steering the opponent out of bounds without giving up additional yardage after contact.

His physicality doesn’t stop at tackling. When the ball is descending from above, McGee forcefully punches or slaps down on the receivers arms right at the intended point where he’d catch the ball, causing receivers to miss catches.

Disciplined progression

In zone coverage, McGee reads the quarterback's eyes before making any decision to challenge the receiver or stay put.

If the quarterback drops back and has his eyes looking downfield, McGee will backpedal into position before engaging the receiver. However, if the quarterback faces away from his side of the field, he’ll take the next few moments to predict where the ball will go.

The tight end or running back will peel away from the offensive line for a check-down pass — which McGee has covered by coming out of the defensive backfield.

On the occasion that the quarterback scrambles outside the pocket for a throw on the run, McGee finds himself in the space between the quarterback and the receiver first before turning around to sprint toward the intended target.

The receiver is usually headed toward the sidelines for a tight catch in-bounds, but McGee can make a play on the ball due to his fluid body control and speed.

College quarterbacks have the accuracy to get the ball in the hands of uncontested receivers, and that’s where McGee’s physical aggression works best. However, if the throw is short, he’ll be there to pick it off and take it the distance the other way.

title: Breaking down the new NCAA 5-for-5 rule, implications for Penn State
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Division I cabinet unanimously approved a change in eligibility rules for student athletes last Tuesday.

The approved “five-for-five” rule now permits athletes competing in college for five years, rather than the previous four years they received. However, there are additional restrictions on it that’ll likely limit collegiate athletes to compete into their late 20s.

Here’s everything to know about the new rule that’ll change collegiate athletics and which Penn State players could take advantage of this.

Rule explained

The new rule states that an athlete's five years begins upon enrollment into their college or the academic year after their 19th birthday, a slight change from the initial proposal after pushback from hockey stakeholders among others.

This becomes a major change that’ll eliminate both redshirts and medical redshirts from the equation. Additionally, it prevents athletes from applying for eligibility extension waivers, something that has become prevalent in college football over the past year.

The 2026-27 season will still feature many players who hold eligibility designations beyond their senior year, but that’ll change after this season. It’ll completely go away for the 2027-28 season when the five-year rule fully goes into effect.

Those listed as seniors will get another year, but players with redshirts will have a fifth year anyways despite not playing for at least one season, so nothing will change with those players.

This rule could change how coaches and programs approach their teams and recruiting. Many players opting to return could leave less roster spots for younger players, something programs will have to work through to still operate successful teams and programs year in and year out.

Penn State players impacted

While many of the underclassmen could make use of this down the line, there’s only six players that could take advantage of it this year. Any player with a redshirt designation will have exhausted that fifth year of eligibility when their senior year comes.

Six Nittany Lions are listed as seniors, but only five of the six have a fifth year of eligibility to use while still playing in 2026, something they wouldn’t have been able to do prior to the rule change.

Quarterback Connor Barry is listed as a senior, but he was in college for four years already prior to joining Penn State. He began in 2022 with Appalachian State, before playing three years with Christopher Newport from 2023-25.

On the other hand, cornerback Zion Tracy, running back Carson Hansen, tight end Benjamin Brahmer, linebacker Kooper Ebel and safety Jamison Patton all could return to the Nittany Lions for the 2027-28 season, using that fifth year of eligibility with the new rule.

All five of those players are likely to make an impact this upcoming season, and with a chance to play for another season, the Nittany Lions could continue to build a veteran team, as many of its starters this year have been in college for multiple years already. There’s still the likelihood that some turn to the NFL after this season, but an additional option that initially wasn’t there could change some minds.

It could also affect recruiting and the transfer portal for Matt Campbell. If someone like Hansen were to return, that means all the running backs this year will be back, barring any transfers. And 4-star Aiden Gibson and 3-star Chukwuma Odoh will be added, as both will join the team next season.

Hansen’s production this season could decide whether he returns. But if he were to return, the running back room becomes even more clogged, making for some interesting decisions for the players and coaching staff.

While coaches have the opportunity to bring back some important players for another year with the new rule, it can also cause change in approach to decisions from the Penn State coaching staff and other programs around the country, depending on choices made by the players impacted by this new rule.

title: State Theatre events from June 29 to July 5
author: Jackson Malenke | The Daily Collegian
description:
State Theatre is hosting movies ranging from musicals, dramas, thrillers and comedies this week.

Here’s what each day has to offer.

“Wicked”

State Theatre is continuing its partnership with Schlow Library by frequently bringing children’s movies to the screen on Wednesdays.

This week, the theatre is showing “Wicked,” a 2024 movie adaptation of the award-winning musical of the same name. The movie follows the Wicked Witch of the West during the events leading up to “The Wizard of Oz.”

The event is free with registration on the website and begins at noon.
